name: Gabon
tagline: From the rainforest hunter-gatherers and the Bantu migrations of the Ogooué basin, through the Atlantic and slave trades of the Myènè coast, the founding of Libreville and French Equatorial Africa, to independence in 1960 and the long Bongo era ended by the coup of 2023.
summary: Gabon straddles the equator on the Atlantic coast of Central Africa, a heavily forested country drained by the Ogooué River. Its earliest inhabitants were rainforest hunter-gatherers — the Babongo and related peoples — followed over the first millennium BCE and after by Bantu-speaking farmers and ironworkers whose movements left some 1,800 rock engravings and dense archaeological remains in the Lopé-Okanda landscape, inscribed by UNESCO in 2007. Coastal Myènè peoples such as the Mpongwe and Orungu, and later the Fang, settled the estuary and rivers and became middlemen in the Atlantic trade. Portuguese navigators reached the estuary in 1472 and named it Gabão; over the following centuries European traders dealt in ivory, dyewoods and enslaved people through coastal kingdoms linked to Orungu and Loango. France signed treaties with Mpongwe rulers from 1839, and in 1849 settled freed captives from a seized slave ship at a site named Libreville. Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza's expeditions opened the Ogooué interior, Gabon became part of French Equatorial Africa in 1910, and Albert Schweitzer founded his hospital at Lambaréné in 1913. Gabon became independent on 17 August 1960 under Léon M'ba; from 1967 it was ruled for over four decades by Omar Bongo, sustained by oil wealth, and then by his son Ali Bongo, until a military coup on 30 August 2023 ended the Bongo family's 56-year hold on power.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: Gabon became an independent republic on 17 August 1960, ending decades as a French colony within French Equatorial Africa. Léon M'ba, who had led the territory's government in the final colonial years, became the country's first head of state and was formally elected president in February 1961. Independence came at the close of a long history: the rainforest hunter-gatherers and Bantu farmers of the Ogooué basin; the Myènè and Fang peoples of the coast and interior; the Portuguese naming of the estuary as Gabão in 1472 and the centuries of Atlantic and slave trades that followed; the founding of Libreville in 1849 as a settlement for liberated captives; Brazza's exploration of the Ogooué; and incorporation into French Equatorial Africa in 1910. Gabon retained close political, military and economic ties to France after independence, and from 1967 entered the long presidency of Omar Bongo.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From France on 17 August 1960, under first president Léon M'ba

quickFacts.label: Capital
quickFacts.value: Libreville, founded 1849 as a settlement for freed slaves; "free town" in French

quickFacts.label: Geography
quickFacts.value: Equatorial Central Africa, mostly rainforest, drained by the Ogooué River; area about 267,667 km²

quickFacts.label: World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Ecosystem and Relict Cultural Landscape of Lopé-Okanda (2007), with c. 1,800 rock engravings

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: Oil-dominated since the 1950s–70s; also manganese and timber; an OPEC member

quickFacts.label: Modern politics
quickFacts.value: Ruled by the Bongo family 1967–2023; a military coup ended that on 30 August 2023

eras.title: Forest peoples and the Bantu migrations
eras.standfirst: The equatorial forest of the Ogooué basin was home to hunter-gatherers such as the Babongo, then to Bantu-speaking farmers and ironworkers whose passage left rock engravings and settlement sites preserved in the Lopé-Okanda landscape.
body: The earliest known inhabitants of what is now Gabon were rainforest hunter-gatherers, the ancestors of peoples such as the Babongo, Baka, Bakola and related groups often grouped together as forest "Pygmies." They lived by hunting, fishing and gathering in the dense equatorial forest, and over time entered into long-standing exchange relationships with farming neighbours, trading meat, honey and forest products. Their presence in the wider Central African forest reaches back many millennia, long before the spread of farming and metalworking.

From the first millennium BCE, and intensively through the first millennium CE, Bantu-speaking peoples moved into the region as part of the vast Bantu expansion that carried agriculture, pottery and ironworking across Central, Eastern and Southern Africa from a homeland near the present Nigeria–Cameroon border. The valley of the Ogooué River — Gabon's principal waterway, running from the southeast to the Atlantic — served as a natural corridor for this migration around the northern fringes of the Congo Basin forests, and successive groups settled its banks, savannas and forest margins.

This deep history is recorded with unusual clarity in the Lopé-Okanda area of central Gabon, where the forest meets relict savanna along the Ogooué. The landscape preserves an exceptional density of habitation sites, evidence of iron-working, and some 1,800 rock engravings (petroglyphs), testifying to the successive passage of different peoples over a very long period. In 2007 UNESCO inscribed the Ecosystem and Relict Cultural Landscape of Lopé-Okanda as a mixed natural and cultural World Heritage Site, citing it as evidence of the Ogooué valley's role as a major route of human migration.

events.title: Forest hunter-gatherers of the Ogooué
events.summary: Rainforest hunter-gatherers, ancestors of peoples such as the Babongo and Baka, inhabited the equatorial forest of present-day Gabon, living by hunting, fishing and gathering and trading with later farming communities.

events.title: Bantu farmers and ironworkers arrive
events.summary: Bantu-speaking peoples moved into the region from the north, bringing farming, pottery and iron-working; the Ogooué valley served as a corridor of the wider Bantu expansion across Central Africa.

events.title: Settlement and iron-working at Lopé-Okanda
events.summary: The Lopé-Okanda landscape along the Ogooué preserves dense habitation sites, iron-working remains and about 1,800 rock engravings, recording the successive passage of peoples through the river corridor.

pullquote.text: The Ogooué valley was a crucial corridor for the migration of Bantu peoples around the fringes of the Congo Basin forests.
pullquote.attribution: Ecosystem and Relict Cultural Landscape of Lopé-Okanda, UNESCO World Heritage

eras.title: The Myènè coast, the Fang, and the Atlantic trade
eras.standfirst: Myènè-speaking peoples such as the Mpongwe and Orungu settled the estuary and rivers and, after Portuguese ships named the coast Gabão in 1472, became middlemen in a trade in ivory, dyewoods and enslaved people linked to Orungu and Loango.
body: From around the start of the second millennium CE, Myènè-speaking Bantu peoples settled the Gabon coast and lower rivers. The Mpongwe established themselves around the estuary (the Gabon Estuary, or Komo) near the future site of Libreville; the Orungu settled at Cape Lopez, where the Ogooué reaches the sea; and the Nkomi occupied the Fernan Vaz lagoon to the south. Organised in clans under chiefs and councils of elders, these coastal communities were well placed to act as intermediaries between the interior and the sea. The Fang, who would become Gabon's largest ethnic group, were a later arrival, migrating into northern and central Gabon largely in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries.

European contact began in 1472, when Portuguese navigators reached the estuary of the Komo River. They are said to have named it Gabão — Portuguese for a hooded cloak or greatcoat — after the shape of the estuary, and the name Gabon derives from this. The Portuguese were followed over the next centuries by Dutch, English and French traders who dealt with the coastal peoples in ivory, ebony and other tropical woods, beeswax and, increasingly, enslaved people. Portions of southern Gabon were loosely linked to the kingdom of Loango, itself associated with the wider Kongo world to the south.

The Atlantic slave trade reached its greatest intensity on this coast between roughly the 1760s and the 1840s, driven by demand from Brazil and Cuba. Coastal Myènè polities — above all the Orungu kingdom at Cape Lopez, alongside Loango further south — operated as middlemen, channelling captives brought from the interior to European and American slavers offshore. By the late eighteenth century several hundred captives a year were being embarked at points described in the records as Gabon and Cape Lopez, and the trade reshaped coastal society and politics before its suppression in the nineteenth century.

events.title: Portuguese reach the estuary and name it Gabão
events.summary: Portuguese navigators arrived at the estuary of the Komo River in 1472 and named it Gabão, the Portuguese word for a hooded cloak, after the estuary's shape; the country's name derives from it.

events.title: Myènè peoples settle the coast
events.summary: Myènè-speaking peoples including the Mpongwe at the estuary, the Orungu at Cape Lopez and the Nkomi at Fernan Vaz settled the Gabon coast, organised in clans well placed to trade with the interior and the sea.

events.title: Peak of the Atlantic slave trade
events.summary: The Atlantic slave trade on the Gabon coast reached its height, driven by Brazilian and Cuban demand; the Orungu kingdom and the Loango coast served as middlemen channelling captives from the interior to European slavers.

events.title: Captives embarked at Gabon and Cape Lopez
events.summary: Records describe roughly 500 captives a year being embarked at points called Gabon and Cape Lopez in the late eighteenth century, an index of the trade run through the coastal Myènè kingdoms.

pullquote.text: The Portuguese named the estuary Gabão, for a hooded cloak — and from that name came Gabon.

eras.title: Libreville, Brazza, and French conquest
eras.standfirst: France signed treaties with Mpongwe rulers from 1839, settled freed captives at Libreville in 1849, and through Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza's Ogooué expeditions extended its rule, folding Gabon into French Equatorial Africa in 1910.
body: French involvement in Gabon was formalised in 1839, when the naval officer Édouard Bouët-Willaumez signed a treaty with the Mpongwe ruler Antchouwe Kowe Rapontchombo, known to the French as King Denis, granting France trading and basing rights on the estuary. American Protestant missionaries established a station at Baraka in 1842. In 1849 the French navy resettled freed Africans taken from a captured slave ship at a site on the estuary; the settlement was named Libreville — "free town" — and grew into the colonial capital and the modern capital of Gabon.

From the 1870s the Italian-born French explorer Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza (1852–1905) opened the interior. His first Ogooué mission (1875–1878) ascended the river largely by negotiation and barter rather than force; his second (1879–1882) carried French influence up the Ogooué and across to the Congo River, where he secured treaties — including with the Téké ruler Makoko — that became the basis of the French Congo. Brazza founded Franceville on the upper Ogooué in 1880 and later served as the French Congo's commissioner-general.

Through the 1880s France consolidated its hold by treaty and administration. An enlarged Gabon was joined to the French Congo in 1886, and Libreville served as the colony's administrative centre. In 1910 the French reorganised their Central African possessions into French Equatorial Africa — a federation of Gabon, the Middle Congo, Ubangi-Shari and Chad, governed from Brazzaville — within which Gabon was constituted as a separate colony. Colonial Gabon's economy rested heavily on the export of timber, especially okoumé, often extracted under harsh conditions of forced and contract labour.

events.title: France treats with King Denis
events.summary: The French officer Bouët-Willaumez signed a treaty in 1839 with the Mpongwe ruler Antchouwe Kowe Rapontchombo ("King Denis"), securing French trading and basing rights on the Gabon Estuary.

events.title: Libreville founded for freed slaves
events.summary: Freed captives taken from a slave ship seized by the French navy were settled on the estuary in 1849; the settlement was named Libreville, "free town," and became the colonial and modern capital.

events.title: Brazza explores the Ogooué
events.summary: Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza led expeditions up the Ogooué and on to the Congo River, founding Franceville in 1880 and signing treaties that opened the interior to French rule.

events.title: Gabon joins French Equatorial Africa
events.summary: France reorganised its Central African colonies into French Equatorial Africa in 1910 — Gabon, the Middle Congo, Ubangi-Shari and Chad, governed from Brazzaville — with Gabon a separate colony.

figures.name: Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza
figures.role: Explorer of the Ogooué and Congo
figures.blurb: Italian-born French explorer whose expeditions up the Ogooué from 1875 and treaties on the Congo opened the interior to French rule; founder of Franceville (1880) and namesake of Brazzaville.

pullquote.text: Freed from a captured slave ship, the first settlers gave their new home a name that was also a promise: Libreville, the free town.

eras.title: Colonial Gabon and the road to independence
eras.standfirst: Within French Equatorial Africa, Gabon's forests fed a timber economy, Albert Schweitzer opened his hospital at Lambaréné in 1913, and a new African political class emerged that carried the colony to independence in 1960.
body: As a colony of French Equatorial Africa, Gabon was governed from Brazzaville and administered through a small French apparatus that relied on African intermediaries and, for decades, on forced and indentured labour. The economy turned on the export of tropical hardwoods — above all okoumé, prized for plywood — cut from the equatorial forest and floated down the Ogooué and lesser rivers to the coast. Population remained sparse and dispersed, and missionary churches, both Catholic and Protestant, became central institutions of colonial society.

In 1913 the Alsatian theologian, organist and physician Albert Schweitzer (1875–1965) and his wife Hélène arrived at Lambaréné, on the Ogooué, and founded a hospital near an existing mission. The Hôpital Albert Schweitzer treated thousands of patients drawn from a wide region and, sustained over half a century, made Lambaréné internationally known; Schweitzer's philosophy of "reverence for life" and his medical work were recognised with the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952.

After the Second World War, France's reforms gave the colonies representation and a measure of self-government, and a Gabonese political class came to the fore. Léon M'ba, a former colonial official and Libreville mayor, and the lawyer Jean-Hilaire Aubame led rival movements. Under the framework of the 1956 loi-cadre and the 1958 French Community, Gabon advanced to internal autonomy as the Gabonese Republic, with M'ba as head of government, setting the stage for full independence.

events.title: Schweitzer founds the Lambaréné hospital
events.summary: Albert Schweitzer and his wife Hélène arrived at Lambaréné on the Ogooué in 1913 and founded a hospital near a mission; it treated thousands and made Lambaréné internationally known. Schweitzer received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952.

events.title: Overseas territory of the French Union
events.summary: After the Second World War Gabon became an overseas territory within the French Union, gaining elected representation and laying the groundwork for African political movements led by figures such as Léon M'ba and Jean-Hilaire Aubame.

events.title: Oil discovered near Port-Gentil
events.summary: Oil was discovered offshore at the Ozouri field near Port-Gentil in 1956; petroleum would become the foundation of independent Gabon's economy and a key source of state revenue.

events.title: Internal autonomy as the Gabonese Republic
events.summary: Under the 1958 French Community Gabon became an autonomous republic with Léon M'ba as head of government, a step short of full sovereignty within the French constitutional framework.

figures.name: Albert Schweitzer
figures.role: Physician, theologian, founder of the Lambaréné hospital
figures.blurb: Alsatian doctor and thinker who in 1913 founded a hospital at Lambaréné on the Ogooué, working there for much of his life; his ethic of "reverence for life" and his medical mission earned the 1952 Nobel Peace Prize.

pullquote.text: At Lambaréné on the Ogooué, Schweitzer built a hospital out of a mission and a conviction he called reverence for life.

eras.title: Independence, oil, and the Bongo era
eras.standfirst: Independent in 1960 under Léon M'ba, Gabon fell after his death to Omar Bongo, who ruled for over four decades on oil wealth and a one-party state; his son Ali Bongo followed, until a military coup in 2023 ended the family's 56-year rule.
body: Gabon became independent on 17 August 1960, with Léon M'ba (1902–1967) as its first leader; he was elected president in February 1961. M'ba's pro-French, increasingly authoritarian rule provoked a coup attempt in February 1964, which French troops swiftly reversed, restoring him to power and underlining the close post-independence partnership between Libreville and Paris. When M'ba died in November 1967, he was succeeded by his vice-president, Albert-Bernard Bongo.

Bongo — who converted to Islam in 1973 and took the name Omar — consolidated control, establishing a single-party state under the Gabonese Democratic Party (PDG) in 1968. His long rule, one of the longest of any African leader, was financed by Gabon's growing oil production, which gave the small, sparsely populated country one of the higher per-capita incomes in sub-Saharan Africa while wealth remained highly concentrated. Under domestic and international pressure, Bongo reintroduced multiparty politics in 1990, but the PDG retained power through contested elections. He died in June 2009, having ruled for over forty years.

Omar Bongo's son Ali Bongo Ondimba won the 2009 presidential election and was re-elected amid disputes in 2016; a stroke in 2018 left lingering questions over his fitness to govern. After a presidential election on 26 August 2023 that authorities said he had won, military officers led by General Brice Clotaire Oligui Nguema seized power in the early hours of 30 August 2023, annulling the result and detaining Ali Bongo. The coup ended the Bongo family's 56-year hold on the presidency; Oligui Nguema was installed as transitional leader, opening an uncertain new chapter in Gabon's politics.

events.title: Independence under Léon M'ba
events.summary: Gabon became an independent republic on 17 August 1960, with Léon M'ba as its first leader; he was formally elected president in February 1961, maintaining close ties to France.

events.title: Coup attempt reversed by France
events.summary: Military officers briefly ousted M'ba in February 1964, but French troops intervened within a day to restore him, illustrating the depth of post-independence Franco-Gabonese ties.

events.title: Omar Bongo takes power
events.summary: On M'ba's death in November 1967 his vice-president Albert-Bernard (later Omar) Bongo became president; in 1968 he made the Gabonese Democratic Party the sole legal party, beginning a four-decade rule.

events.title: Death of Omar Bongo; Ali Bongo succeeds
events.summary: Omar Bongo died in June 2009 after ruling for over forty years; his son Ali Bongo Ondimba won the ensuing presidential election, continuing the family's hold on power.

events.title: Military coup ends Bongo rule
events.summary: After a disputed 26 August 2023 election, officers led by General Brice Oligui Nguema seized power on 30 August 2023, annulling the result and detaining Ali Bongo — ending the Bongo family's 56-year hold on the presidency.

figures.name: Léon M'ba
figures.blurb: Former colonial official and Libreville mayor who led Gabon to independence and became its first president in 1961; pro-French and authoritarian, he was restored by French troops after a 1964 coup and died in office in 1967.

figures.name: Omar Bongo
figures.blurb: Succeeded M'ba in 1967 and ruled for over four decades through a one-party then dominant-party state funded by oil; converted to Islam in 1973, taking the name Omar. One of Africa's longest-serving leaders.

pullquote.text: The coup of 30 August 2023 ended fifty-six years in which a single family held the presidency of Gabon.

figures.name: Pierre Savorgnan de Brazza
figures.blurb: Italian-born French explorer whose Ogooué expeditions from 1875 and treaties on the Congo opened Gabon's interior to French rule; founder of Franceville and namesake of Brazzaville.

figures.name: Albert Schweitzer
figures.role: Physician and founder of the Lambaréné hospital
figures.blurb: Physician and theologian who founded a hospital at Lambaréné in 1913 and worked there for much of his life; awarded the 1952 Nobel Peace Prize for his ethic of reverence for life.

figures.name: Omar Bongo
figures.blurb: Ruled Gabon for more than four decades on oil wealth and a dominant-party system, among the longest-serving leaders in Africa; succeeded on his 2009 death by his son Ali Bongo.
